title: Life Portfolio Design

content.question: Which life area are you neglecting that will catch up with you in 3 years?
sop: List your core life areas: health, relationships, finances, creativity, career, personal growth.
Rate each area's Diversification (how many different areas you're actually invested in) and Balance (how evenly spread that investment is), 1-10 each (see calculation, page 5).
Identify the lowest-scoring area, that's your concentration risk.
Schedule one recurring weekly action for that area, treated with the same non-negotiable priority as work.
Rebalance quarterly, a portfolio only stays diversified if you keep checking it.
questions: Which life area are you neglecting that will catch up with you in 3 years?
If your business disappeared tomorrow, what else in your life would still be standing?
Are you as diversified in health, relationships, and identity as you are in income?

definitions.def: The danger of having too much riding on a single area, source, or bet. If that one thing fails, everything is exposed; spreading out reduces it.

content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10: Diversification (spread across life areas), Balance (no single area dominating). Diversification 6 times Balance 7 equals 42. Higher means your life can absorb a hit to one area without collapsing; lower means everything depends on one area holding up.
content.closing_statement: Don't let one life area carry all the weight.

caption:
Principle Nº 184: Life Portfolio Design

Your life should be diversified.

Ask yourself: Which life area are you neglecting that will catch up with you in 3 years?
